segments.s626-g9: This is a nominal question phrase in classical/poetic word order: ز + گیتی introduces the domain ('in/from the world'), followed by the comparative adjective زیانکارتر modifying کار ('a more harmful/ruinous deed'), and it ends with the copula چی ست ('what is'). ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s626-g10: که begins a relative clause ('that/which'). بر is a preposition 'on/upon' used here with کرده (the perfect participle 'done'); کرده is a participial/deverbal adjective 'done' referring back to کار. او is the clitic pronoun 'he/it' (object of کرده). بباید is a modal auxiliary 'one must/ought to', and گریست is the infinitive/masdar-like verbal noun 'to weep' or 'lament' — together meaning 'that one must weep (for what) he has done.'
segments.s626-g11: چه دانی تو is a direct interrogative 'what do you know' (چه = 'what'); دانی is 2nd‑person singular present 'you know'. اندر جهان سودمند is a postpositional phrase 'in the world (that is) beneficial', with سودمند functioning as the adjective/modifier of the understood 'thing'.
segments.s626-g12: که introduces the relative/result clause 'which/so that'. از + کردنش uses از with the verbal noun کردن ('doing'); the enclitic ش = 'its/his' attaches to کردن to mean 'doing it'. مرد is 'man' and گردد is the 3rd‑person (subjunctive/present) of گردیدن 'become' used here to state a general result ('becomes'). بلند is the predicate adjective 'lofty/exalted'.
segments.s626-g13: فرستاده گفت is a simple past narrative 'the messenger said'. آنک functions as a conjunction/relative 'that' introducing the reported content. دانا بود is a past-tense predicate 'was wise' (دانا = adjective 'wise', بود = past copula).
segments.s626-g14: This is a continuation describing the person: همیشه ('always') modifies the subsequent predicate. بزرگ و توانا are coordinate adjectives 'great and powerful', and بود is again the past copula 'was', giving 'he was always great and able/powerful.'
segments.s626-g15: تن مرد نادان is a noun phrase 'the body/person of an ignorant man' (تن used for 'person'). The comparative phrase uses ز as 'than' (classical comparative 'z ... ... tar'): ز گل خوارتر means 'lower/more despicable than a clay‑/mud‑eater'; خوارتر is the comparative form (‑تر) of خوار 'despicable/low'.
segments.s626-g16: به هر نیکئی is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding every good' (به = 'with/regarding', هر = 'each/any', نیکئی = 'goodness/good deed'). ناسزاوارتر is another comparative adjective 'more unworthy / deserving of insult', so the clause states he is even more unfit for any good.
segments.s626-g41: This conditional clause begins with the conjunction 'اگر' (if). 'باژ خواهی' is a present-tense desiderative construction: 'باژ' (an archaic/poetic word meaning tribute—see senses note) plus 'خواهی' ('you want' / 'you would desire'). 'ز قیصر' (from Caesar) uses the poetic preposition 'ز' before the name 'قیصر'. The predicate is the verbless copular adjective phrase 'رواست' = 'rوا + ست', where 'روا' means 'permissible/fitting' and 'ست' is the copula 'is' (poetic contraction), so the whole means 'it is permissible.'
segments.s626-g42: The particle 'ک' is the clitic form of 'که' introducing a causal/explanatory clause ('for'/'because'). 'دستور تو' = 'your decree/command' (noun + possessive). 'بر جهان' uses the preposition 'بر' with the meaning 'over' or 'upon' (indicating dominion). The final word 'پادشاست' is a nominal/ornamental predicate meaning 'is (a) reign/sovereignty' or 'is kingly' — here used to state that your command is sovereign over the world.
segments.s626-g43: This short clause uses the poetic preposition 'ز' meaning 'because of' or 'from' to mark cause: 'ز گفتار او' = 'because of his speech/words.' The predicate 'شاد شد' is past tense compound 'شاد' (happy) + 'شد' (became/was made), so 'the king became glad.' 'شهریار' is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for a slightly elevated word order typical of epic poetry.
segments.s626-g44: 'دلش تازه شد' is another past-tense result: 'دل' (heart) + enclitic possessive 'ش' (his) + 'تازه شد' ('became fresh/new'). The simile introduced by 'چو' ('like/as') compares the heart to 'گل' (a flower); 'اندر بهار' ('in spring') completes the image. Word order places the possessed noun before the verb ('his heart became') and then the comparative phrase.
segments.s626-g45: 'برون شد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'went out' or 'issued forth' (literally 'became outside'). 'فرستاده' is the agent noun 'messenger' placed after the verb in the typical narrative order. 'از پیش شاه' literally 'from before the king' = 'from the king's presence'; here 'پیش' carries the meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before (someone),' so the whole line says the messenger departed the king's presence.
segments.s626-g46: This is a compact two-part narrative: 'شب آمد' ('night came' or 'night fell') followed by 'برآمد درفش سیاه' where 'برآمد' = 'rose/appeared' and 'درفش سیاه' = 'the black banner/standard.' Verbs are simple past tense; the juxtaposition of two clauses without conjunction is a common epic device to speed the scene.
segments.s626-g47: 'پدید آمد' is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'appeared' or 'came into view' (pādid + amad). 'آن چادر' = 'that veil/tent' (demonstrative + noun); in this poetic context 'چادر' functions as 'veil' rather than 'camp tent.' 'مشکبوی' is an adjectival compound meaning 'musk-scented' and modifies 'چادر.' The word order (verb before demonstrative phrase) is common in Persian poetic narration.
segments.s626-g48: 'به عنبر بیالود خورشید روی' is an ablative/instrumental descriptive phrase: 'به عنبر' ('with/by ambergris'), 'بیالود' modifies that relation (the exact nuance is poetic: 'tinged/mixed/imbued' — the lexical senses given in the word list don't precisely match the poetic usage, see senses), and 'خورشید روی' is a compound epithet 'sun-faced' (خورشید = sun/splendor, روی = face). Grammatically the phrase describes the appearance of the face as perfumed or tinged with ambergris beneath the musk-scented veil.
segments.s626-g49: This is compressed, almost two short clauses run together: 'شکیبا نبد' uses the archaic/poetic negative-past 'نبد' (= نبُود) after 'شکیبا' (patience / long‑suffering) to state 'there was no patience.' Immediately following is the nominal phrase 'گنبد تیزگرد' = 'the swiftly‑turning dome' (an epithet for the vault of heaven); word order is noun + adjective (noun first, adjective after).
segments.s626-g50: 'سر خفته' is a noun phrase 'the sleeping head' where 'سر' (head) stands for the chief/leader (the king) and 'خفته' is the adjective 'asleep.' The verb phrase 'از خواب بیدار کرد' uses 'از' = 'from/out of' + 'خواب' = 'sleep' and the causative/periphrastic past 'کرد' meaning 'caused (to become) awake' — i.e. 'awoke (him) from sleep.' The subject is supplied by the previous phrase ('the dome').
segments.s626-g51: This clause is compressed: 'درفشی بزد' literally 'a banner/standard was struck' (بزد = struck/struck up), and 'چشمه آفتاب' 'the spring/source of the sun' qualifies the striking — a poetic image of the sunrise as a fountain whose light 'strikes' a banner. Word order is object/phrase + verb + modifier (poetic inversion).
segments.s626-g52: 'سرِ شاهِ گیتی' (here written without overt ezāfe) is a genitival chain 'the head of the king of the world' (head = chief/leader). 'سبک شد' is a two‑word predicate 'became light' (سبک = light in weight/relieved; شد = became). 'ز خواب' = 'from/on account of sleep' — together meaning he was relieved of sleep (i.e. awakened).
segments.s626-g53: 'در بار' = 'in the court/royal hall' (در = in; بار here = occasion/court), then 'بگشاد' is used as a past action 'opened/held' (بگشاد = گشود) and 'سالار بار' is a compound 'the master/commander of the court' (سالار + بار). So the whole phrase states that in the hall the court's master opened the affair/ceremony.
segments.s626-g54: نشست here is the past intransitive 'sat (down)'. The prepositional phrase 'از بر تخت خود' is an older/formal locative meaning 'upon his throne' (از بر + تخت), with 'خود' emphasising 'his own.' 'شهریار' names the subject/agent 'the king.' Together: 'the king sat upon his own throne.'
segments.s626-g55: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/dignified past 'he commanded/said' and is followed by the subordinating 'تا' = 'so that/in order that.' The clause 'خلعت آراستند' uses 'خلعت' (robe of honour) as direct object and 'آراستند' (they adorned/prepared) as the verb: 'he ordered that they prepare the robe.'
segments.s626-g56: 'فرستاده را' with the object marker 'را' indicates 'the envoy' as the object; 'پیش او' = 'before him/in his presence' (پیش = in the presence of) and 'خواستند' here means 'they requested/asked for' — idiomatically 'they summoned him.' The verb is plural past 'they asked/summoned.'
segments.s626-g57: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing items that confer status. The sequence uses repeated و ('and') to coordinate a string of valuables: زرین (an adjective used as 'golden' or 'gold'), اسپ 'steed/horse', and ستام (here presented as a noun in the list). The word order is a simple coordination typical of classical Persian: preposition + list of items joined by و.
segments.s626-g58: ز again marks source/possession ('from' / 'of') before دینار گیتی 'the world's dinars' (دینار here a monetary item, گیتی 'the world'). که introduces a relative clause 'that/which', and بردند is past-tense 3rd-person plural 'they took/gained' (بردن can mean both 'take' and 'gain'); نام is the object meaning 'name' in the sense of reputation or renown. Together the clause says they 'gained name/reputation' from those worldly dinars.
segments.s626-g59: The opening ز marks a list of luxuries: دینار (money/coin) and گوهر (jewel), then another ز introduces a second pair مشک (musk) and عبیر (fragrance/ambergris). The repeated use of ز is a poetic device enumerating sources of wealth and scent; و simply links the paired nouns.
segments.s626-g60: فزون گشت is a two-word verbal phrase: فزون ('greater/more') with گشت (past of گشتن 'to become/turn') yields 'became greater/increased'. از before اندیشه introduces the comparison/source ('than/from thought' — i.e., beyond what thought imagines). اندیشه is 'thought/imagination'; تیزویر is an obscure compound/name standing in genitive relation to اندیشه ('the thought of Tizvir' or 'Tizvir's imagining'). The whole clause means their abundance exceeded imagination.
segments.s626-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard' (verb before subject, a common epic order); رومی is the subject 'the Roman' (the one who heard). پسند آمدش is an idiomatic construction: پسند 'liking/approval' + آمدش 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic -ش = 'to him'), together meaning 'it pleased him' or 'he liked it'.
segments.s626-g34: سخنهای او is a possessive noun phrase 'his words' (سخن + plural ـهای, او = 'his'). سودمند آمدش repeats the same idiom as above: سودمند 'useful' + آمدش 'came to him' = 'he found (them) useful' or 'they seemed useful to him.' The verb/verb-like آمدش is again 3rd-person with enclitic referring back to the experiencer.
segments.s626-g35: بخندید is 3rd-person past 'he laughed'. و is the coordinating 'and' linking two actions. بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the king' (بر = toward/to here). کرد is the past tense of 'to do/make' used as a light verb, and آفرین is the object/word made — 'acclamation' or 'bravo' (a noun). Together 'کرد آفرین' = 'made an acclamation.'
segments.s626-g36: بدو is the dative/clitic phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'). گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so no listed sense matches—see senses mapping). فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' functioning as the content of what was said, and ایران‌زمین is the noun 'land of Iran' (the phrase is the invoked blessing: 'Blessed (be) Iran-land').
segments.s626-g37: که introduces indirect/discoursal content ('that' / 'what he said'). تخت شهنشاه is a genitival noun phrase 'the throne of the Shahanshah' (تخت = throne, شهنشاه = king of kings). بیند is the present/optative form of 'to see' here used in an optative/habitual sense ('may see' / 'sees') often found in epic style; همی is an iterative/continuous particle ('ever/always').
segments.s626-g38: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. موبد is the subject 'the mobed' (Zoroastrian priest). بروبر is an adverbial or prepositional phrase meaning 'face-to-face' / 'opposite' (i.e. sitting opposite someone). نشیند is the present/habitual 'sits' and همی again marks continuity/habit ('when the mobed sits opposite').
segments.s626-g39: به دانش is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/through knowledge' (به used adverbially). جهان را is a definite object 'the world' (را marks the object). بلند افسری is a noun phrase 'a lofty diadem/helmet' (بلند modifies افسری). The clause is elliptical/poetic: the action 'he raised/made' is implied (a common epic verbless or compact construction where 'به دانش' indicates the means).
segments.s626-g40: به موبد is the dative 'to the mobed' (he conferred something on the priest). ز هر مهتری uses ز + هر to mean 'over/from every elder/chief' in a comparative sense, and برتری is the noun 'superiority'—together 'superiority above every chief'. The clause states that he granted the mobed preeminence relative to all other elders/chiefs.
segments.s626-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The phrase orders the elements poetically: subject خورشید ('the sun') + locative phrase بر چرخ ('upon the sky/heaven') + verb بنمود ('showed/revealed') + object دست ('hand'); بنمود is the past (preterite) form of نمودن (an older poetic verb meaning 'show, manifest'), and دست is a pictorial noun here (the sun 'showed/extended its hand' = shone/revealed itself).
segments.s626-g2: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king of kings' (title). بر تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase 'on the golden throne' (بر + noun + adjective). نشست is the simple past (3rd person singular) of نشستن 'to sit' — here 'took his seat/sat' — placed at the end as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s626-g3: فرستاده قیصر is a genitival/attributive sequence 'the messenger of Caesar' (classical style without an explicit ezāfe). آمد is the past tense 'came'. به در is an idiomatic locative 'to the court / to the royal presence' (در here = gate/court). The clause is S (subject) + V + locative adverbial.
segments.s626-g4: خرد یافته is a compound adjective: خرد + یافته (past participle) = 'having acquired wisdom' = 'wise/experienced'. This participial adjective modifies موبد (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest). پرگهر here is a proper name (Par‑gohar) identifying the mōbed; poetry often omits linking particles, so موبد پرگهر = 'the mōbed Par‑gohar'.
segments.s626-g5: به پیش شهنشاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the presence of the shahanshah' (به + پیش = 'to the presence of'). رفتند is the past tense (3rd plural) 'they went'. شاد follows the verb and functions as an adverbial/predicative adjective 'joyful/cheerful', so 'they went joyful' = 'they went in high spirits.'
segments.s626-g6: سخنها 'the speeches/words' is the topic (plural). ز هرگونه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'of/from every kind' (ز = 'of/from', هرگونه = 'every kind'). کردند یاد is a light-verb construction: یاد (noun 'remembrance/mention') + کردن (here past کردند) = 'they recalled/mentioned'. Word order here puts the object/topic before the light-verb predicate, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s626-g7: فرستاده را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker marking 'the messenger' as the addressee. موبد is the subject 'the mōbed (priest)'. شاه placed after موبد indicates the setting 'in the presence of the king' (poetic omission of a preposition) or can mark that these words are spoken before the shah. گفت is the past tense (3rd singular) of گفتن 'said'. The sequence object + subject + setting + گفت is a common poetic order and indicates the mōbed spoke to the messenger in front of the king.
segments.s626-g8: که is the complementizer introducing reported/quoted speech here and is tightly joined to the vocative ای (written here as که‌ای). ای مرد is the direct vocative 'O man'. هشیار 'alert' is an adjectival attribute; بی‌یار 'without a friend/companion' and جفت 'pair/mate' together mean 'without companion or match'. The adjectives follow the vocative in a straightforward enumerative description of the addressee.
segments.s626-g25: بدو (a prepositional enclitic) = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used in indirect speech) — note the supplied senses list lacked the verbal sense, so treat it as the 3rd-person past. موبد is the speaker 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest). که introduces the reported content. اندیشه کن is an imperative: اندیشه (noun-stem 'thought/thinking') combined with the light verb کن (imperative 'do') yields the command 'think' — classical Persian often forms imperatives this way.
segments.s626-g26: کز is the conjunction 'that/from which' introducing the explanatory/result clause. اندیشه is the noun 'thought' ('by thought' or 'through thinking' here). بازیب is the verbal stem meaning 'recover/restore' and combines with the auxiliary/verb گردد (become) to yield a passive/result sense 'is recovered/restored.' Note the poetic word order: the verb complex (بازیب گردد) governs the following noun سخن, 'speech,' which is the thing that will be restored.
segments.s626-g27: ز گیتی = 'of/from the world' (ز + گیتی), a poetic way to say 'in the world.' هرانکو is an indefinite 'whoever' (classical form). بی‌آزارتر is the adjective بی‌آزار ('harmless/inoffensive') with the comparative suffix -تر, meaning 'more harmless.' The phrase is an attributive description 'whoever in the world is more harmless.'
segments.s626-g28: چنان دان is an instruction 'consider (him) thus' — دان here functions as 'consider/think' (imperative or jussive). که introduces the explanatory clause. مرگش = 'his death' (مرگ + enclitic possessive). زیانکارتر is زیانکار ('harmful/detrimental') in the comparative form -تر, 'more harmful' — the copula is omitted, so literally 'that his death (is) more harmful.'
segments.s626-g29: به مرگ = 'with regard to death' or 'to/for death.' بد‌ان here is a fused classical form meaning 'at that/of it' (originally 'به آن'); in the supplied segmentation it appears as ب + دان. شاد باشی is a jussive/optative expression 'you should be glad' (شاد = happy; باشی = subjunctive/imperative form of بودن). رواست is a predicate 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا + است, with the copula contracted), stating that such gladness is appropriate. Overall the clause asserts that being glad at death is fitting.
segments.s626-g30: چو introduces a causal/temporal clause 'when/since.' زاید (from زاد) here means 'comes forth/arises' (classical sense 'is produced' or 'issues forth'), referring to what issues from the body. بد و نیک تن = 'the bad and the good of the body' (بد و نیک modifying تن; classical juxtaposition), i.e. the body's bad and good aspects. مرگ راست = 'death (is) right/just' — again the copula is omitted, a terse predication common in poetry.
segments.s626-g31: ازین = از + این ('from this'). سودمندی = 'usefulness/advantage.' بود is the past copula 'was' (here used to state that from this usefulness there resulted that harm). زان = 'from that' (classical 'از آن') introduces the resulting harm. زیان = 'loss/harm' — the clause states a causal relation: 'from this benefit there was that loss.'
segments.s626-g32: خرد را = 'wisdom' marked with the definite/object marker را, here the thing to be put forward. می‌انجی (reading as میانجی) means 'mediator' or 'mediate' — in context it's an imperative construction: میانجی کن (make/appoint as mediator). کن is the imperative 'do/make.' اندر میان = 'in the middle / among them.' So the clause commands: 'Make wisdom the mediator in between.'
segments.s626-g17: ز is the prepositional particle 'from' marking source; نادان و دانا is a coordinated noun phrase 'the ignorant and the wise'. زدی is 2nd-person singular past of زدن here used idiomatically to mean 'recite/perform/tell', and داستان is the direct object 'a tale/story'. Word order is somewhat inverted and poetic (source phrase + verb + object).
segments.s626-g18: شنیدی is 2nd-person singular past 'did you hear'. مگر here is the rhetorical interrogative 'is it not?/did you by any chance?', introducing the question. پاسخ راستان is a noun phrase 'the reply/answer of the righteous' (راستان = 'the upright/the righteous').
segments.s626-g19: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (introducing the indirect object). گفت is past 'said' and موبد is the subject 'the mōbed/priest' (classical inversion places the verb before or adjacent to the subject). که introduces the content of speech; نیکو here is an adverb 'well/carefully' modifying the imperative that follows, and نگر is the imperative 'look/regard/consider'.
segments.s626-g20: بیندیش is an imperative 'reflect/think', and و simply links two commands. ماهی is the object 'a fish'; به + خشکی expresses direction 'onto/into the dry (land)' and مبر is the negative imperative 'do not take/carry'. The clause is a conventional admonition formed with imperatives and a negative imperative.
segments.s626-g21: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger' and گفت is the past 'said'. ای introduces a vocative; پسندیده مرد is the vocative noun phrase 'chosen/esteemed man' addressed by the speaker.
segments.s626-g22: سخن‌ها ('words/speeches') is the topical subject (plural). ز دانش 'from knowledge' marks source; توان is a modal 'one can/can' and combines with the following verbal noun/phrase یاد کرد ('to recall/remember'), with کرد functioning as the light verb. The construction expresses ability: 'words from knowledge can be remembered.'
segments.s626-g23: تو is the emphatic second-person pronoun 'you'. این‌گر is a conditional particle 'if this is so / if that is the case'. دگرگونه modifies دانی: 'differently/otherwise you know' (دانی = you know, present). بگوی is an optative/imperative-like form 'say/tell' used here to command or request expression of that contrary knowledge.
segments.s626-g24: که introduces the result or content clause: 'that'. از دانش 'by/from knowledge' marks cause or source. افزون شود is a verbal phrase where افزون ('increased') with شود ('becomes') yields 'may be increased' or 'may grow', and آبروی is the subject 'reputation/honor'. The whole clause means 'so that reputation may be increased by knowledge.'

sentences.s626-s5: What deed in the world is more ruinous than one for which one must weep over what he has done?
sentences.s626-s6: Do you know of any beneficial thing in the world by doing which a man becomes exalted?
sentences.s626-s7: The messenger said that he was wise; he was always great and powerful.
sentences.s626-s8: The body (or person) of an ignorant man is meaner than a mud‑eater; with respect to every good he is yet more unworthy.
sentences.s626-s21: If you desire tribute from Caesar, it is permissible; for your command rules over the world.
sentences.s626-s22: The king was made glad by his words; his heart became fresh like a flower in spring.
sentences.s626-s23: A messenger went out from the king's presence; night came and the black banner appeared.
sentences.s626-s24: There appeared that musk-scented veil; the sun-faced one was tinged with amber.
sentences.s626-s25: There was no patience; the swiftly turning dome roused the sleeping head from sleep.
sentences.s626-s26: A banner was struck from the sun's spring; the head of the king of the world grew light from sleep.
sentences.s626-s27: In the court the master of the household opened (the proceedings); the king sat upon his own throne.
sentences.s626-s28: He commanded that a robe be prepared; they called the envoy before him.
sentences.s626-s29: They gained renown from silver and gold, from steeds and stām, and from the dinars of the world.
sentences.s626-s30: From dinars and jewels, from musk and fragrance, their abundance increased beyond (what) thought could (reach) — beyond Tizvir's imagining.
sentences.s626-s17: When the Roman heard, it pleased him; he found his words useful.
sentences.s626-s18: He laughed and made an acclamation to the king; to him he said, 'Blessed is the land of Iran.'
sentences.s626-s19: That the Shahanshah may always behold the throne, when the mobed sits opposite him.
sentences.s626-s20: By learning he raised the world's lofty diadem; to the mobed he gave superiority over every elder.
sentences.s626-s1: When the sun displayed its hand upon the sky, the king of kings took his seat on the golden throne.
sentences.s626-s2: Caesar's messenger came to the court, having found the wise mōbed Par-gohar.
sentences.s626-s3: They went before the king of kings, joyful; they recalled words of every kind.
sentences.s626-s4: The mōbed said to the messenger in the king's presence: 'O man, alert, friendless and without a mate.'
sentences.s626-s13: The mobed said to him, "Think — for by thought speech will be restored."
sentences.s626-s14: Of everyone in the world who is the more harmless, consider him so that his death is the more harmful.
sentences.s626-s15: With respect to death, it is fitting that you be glad of it; when bad and good issue from the body, death is just.
sentences.s626-s16: From this usefulness there was that harm; make wisdom the mediator in between.
sentences.s626-s9: You recited tales from the ignorant and the wise; did you hear the reply of the righteous?
sentences.s626-s10: The mōbed said to him, 'Look well, reflect, and do not carry a fish onto dry land.'
sentences.s626-s11: The messenger said, 'O chosen man, one can recall words from knowledge.'
sentences.s626-s12: If you know otherwise, you should say it, so that reputation may grow from knowledge.
